content:
The patient presented with a three-week history of exertional chest discomfort and dyspnea on exertion. Initial electrocardiogram demonstrated ST-segment depression in the anterior leads, and serial troponin measurements were negative, consistent with unstable angina. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild hypokinesis of the anterior wall with an ejection fraction of 48 percent. Coronary angiography was performed on hospital day two, which identified a 90 percent stenosis of the mid-left anterior descending artery with TIMI II flow. The patient underwent successful percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a drug-eluting stent to the mid-LAD, achieving TIMI III flow and less than 10 percent residual stenosis. The procedure was uncomplicated with no dissection, perforation, or no-reflow phenomenon. Angiography of the right coronary artery and left circumflex showed no significant disease.

Following the intervention, the patient remained hemodynamically stable on dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and ticagrelor. Serial troponin levels remained negative post-procedure. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography on day four demonstrated improved anterior wall motion with ejection fraction now estimated at 52 percent. The patient was discharged on hospital day five in stable condition, chest pain-free, and tolerating oral intake without difficulty. He was counseled extensively on medication adherence, particularly the importance of continuing dual antiplatelet therapy for a minimum of twelve months, and instructed on cardiac risk factor modification including smoking cessation, dietary sodium restriction, and a supervised cardiac rehabilitation program. Follow-up appointments were arranged with cardiology in two weeks and with his primary care physician within one week of discharge.
